summary: DHH announces that Workfeed, a Danish employee scheduling startup he invested in, is expanding to the American market. During his three years in Denmark, he invested in five local startups on the premise of building profitable businesses without venture capital. Workfeed has already achieved profitability in Denmark, becoming the top-rated scheduling system there. The post explains how employee scheduling software is uniquely localized due to payroll integrations and labor law requirements, making international expansion harder than typical SaaS. DHH promotes their US launch by offering three months of free access to American service businesses willing to provide feedback.
key_points: Employee scheduling software is surprisingly localized due to payroll integrations, labor laws, and industry standards, unlike universal tools like Basecamp
Workfeed proved the bootstrap model works by becoming profitable and market-leading in Denmark without venture capital
Denmark's small market (under 6 million people) creates a natural ceiling that forces successful startups to expand internationally
DHH invested in five Danish startups specifically on anti-VC terms: profitability-focused, staying local, avoiding the venture capital path
money_quotes: All on the premise that we'd work towards becoming profitable, remaining in the country, and avoiding the VC timebomb.
That might sound pretty boring, but if you run or work at a restaurant still trying to do things with Excel spreadsheets or call sheets, you know this can be a huge pain and time suck.
That's exactly the kind of drudgery that software was meant to solve.
What I found interesting about employee scheduling software is how localized much of it is.
It's quite unlike the universality afforded Jason and I working on something like Basecamp.
It's a way to help a Danish startup fulfill the hypothesis that it's possible to build awesome SaaS solutions without going down the venture rabbit hole.
key_insight: Bootstrapped, profitable SaaS companies can compete and expand internationally without venture capital, even in highly localized markets like employee scheduling.
